claim.study_type: Observational (cross-sectional and longitudinal online survey studies)

data.source: Online self-report questionnaires administered to Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk) workers in the United States
data.wave_or_subset: Study 1: March 19–20 2020 (with subsample surveyed in Oct 2019 pre-pandemic); Study 2 and Study 3: additional cross-sectional and 1-week follow-up waves
data.sample_size: 1,195 participants across three studies (e.g., Study 1: 399 recruited / 346 completed; 193 with pre-pandemic data)
data.notes: Data collected entirely online; convenience MTurk sample limited to U.S. adults

method.description: Researchers created a 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale to measure two dimensions of threat (realistic vs. symbolic). They collected survey data from three MTurk samples, performed exploratory (PCA) and confirmatory factor analyses (CFA) to establish the two-factor structure, tested longitudinal measurement invariance, and used structural equation modelling (SEM) and regressions to examine how each threat dimension predicted psychological distress, well-being, and adherence to public-health behaviours.
steps: Design 10 survey items reflecting realistic and symbolic COVID-19 threats.
Recruit U.S. MTurk workers (three separate studies, one with a pre-pandemic baseline).
Administer threat items plus external scales (need for closure, belief in a dangerous world, affect, anxiety, life satisfaction, public-health adherence, social-identity affirmation).
Conduct Principal Component Analysis to explore factor structure.
Run Confirmatory Factor Analysis to confirm two-factor model and test configural, metric, and scalar invariance over time.
Evaluate reliability and convergent/divergent validity of each subscale.
Use Structural Equation Modelling / regressions to link threat dimensions to outcomes (anxiety, avoidance, intrusion, affect, guideline adherence, policy support).
Interpret results and compare effects of realistic vs. symbolic threats.
method.models: Principal Component Analysis, Confirmatory Factor Analysis, Longitudinal CFA (measurement invariance), Structural Equation Modelling, Linear regressions (reporting unstandardised b-coefficients)
method.outcome_variable: Multiple psychological and behavioural outcomes (e.g., anxiety, COVID-19 impact intrusion and avoidance, life satisfaction, adherence to CDC guidelines, support for public-health initiatives)
method.independent_variables: Realistic COVID-19 threat score, Symbolic COVID-19 threat score
method.control_variables: Pre-pandemic anxiety (in longitudinal sample); political ideology; personality/value measures such as need for cognitive closure and belief in a dangerous world (controls in some SEM models)

results.summary: Both realistic and symbolic COVID-19 threats were associated with higher psychological distress, but they showed distinct patterns: realistic threat predicted greater anxiety, intrusive thoughts, and higher adherence to public-health guidelines, whereas symbolic threat predicted greater avoidance and intrusion, lower support for public-health measures, but increased positive affect via social-identity affirming behaviours.

metadata.original_paper_title: Measuring Two Distinct Psychological Threats of COVID-19 and their Unique Impacts on Well-being and Adherence to Public Health Behaviors
